questions.prompt: Your site has the exact same content at four URLs: http://example.com, http://www.example.com, https://example.com, and https://www.example.com. You haven't set canonicals or redirects. What does Google do?

choices.text: Penalizes the site for duplicate content.
Picks one URL as the canonical (using signals like HTTPS preference and internal linking) and files the others as alternates. The 'duplicate content penalty' is a myth.
Refuses to index any of the four URLs.
Indexes all four and treats them as separate pages forever.

questions.explanation: Google clusters near-identical pages and picks one canonical per cluster. There is no penalty for duplicate content (Google says so explicitly) — duplicates are just inefficient, because Google might pick a canonical you didn't want. The fix is to pick one canonical and 301-redirect the other three to it.

questions.prompt: You want to keep a page out of Google's search results. Which approach actually works?

choices.text: Block the URL in robots.txt with Disallow.
Add a noindex meta tag and leave the page crawlable so Google can read the noindex.
Both A and B — they reinforce each other.
Delete the page from your CMS.

questions.explanation: noindex is the directive that removes a page from search results, but Google has to crawl the page to see the noindex tag. If you block the URL in robots.txt, Googlebot never reads the noindex — and the URL can still appear in results as a bare link. Use noindex alone, not robots.txt.
